whatItDoes: Browse, search, and pick emojis from a categorized grid with 9 categories including Smileys, People, Animals, Food, Travel, Activities, Objects, Symbols, and Flags. Features search filtering, click-to-copy, and a recently used history stored in localStorage.
whyItExists: Finding the right emoji can be tedious when scrolling through hundreds of options; this tool organizes them into browsable categories with search and keeps track of your most recently used emojis for quick access.
whoShouldUse: Developers, content creators, designers, and anyone who needs to quickly find and copy emojis for use in code, messages, social media, or documents.
useCases: Quickly copy an emoji for use in code comments, commit messages, or UI text
Browse emojis by category to find the perfect one for a project or presentation
Search for a specific emoji when you know roughly what it looks like
Access recently used emojis without searching through the full library
Discover new emojis across different categories
Copy flag emojis for internationalization or locale indicators
instructions: Type in the search input to filter emojis across all categories
Click a category tab to browse emojis within that specific category
Click any emoji to copy it to your clipboard
Use the Recent tab to quickly access emojis you've recently copied
The copied confirmation shows which emoji was copied to your clipboard
Scroll through the grid if the category has more emojis than fit on screen
examples: Click the Smileys category, then click the grinning face emoji to copy it
Type "heart" in the search to find heart-related emojis across all categories
Open the Flags category to browse country flag emojis for internationalization
bestPractices: Use the search feature when you know what emoji you want instead of browsing categories
Check the Recent tab for frequently used emojis to save time
Remember that emoji rendering varies across platforms and operating systems
Use flag emojis sparingly in UI as they may not display consistently on all devices
Copy multiple emojis by clicking them one at a time for use in sequences
commonMistakes: Expecting emojis to look the same across all platforms — rendering varies by OS and browser
Using skin-tone modified emojis without testing on target platforms
Overusing emojis in professional or formal content where they may be distracting
Assuming all emojis are universally supported on older devices or browsers
Not checking that copied emojis paste correctly into the target application
faq: Why do emojis look different on my computer vs my phone? — Emoji appearance varies by operating system and platform. Each vendor (Apple, Google, Microsoft) has their own emoji designs.
Can I search by emoji name? — The search filters the displayed grid. Type keywords like "heart", "star", or "food" to narrow down options.
Where are my recently used emojis stored? — They are saved in your browser's localStorage and persist between sessions until you clear browser data.
How do I copy an emoji? — Simply click on any emoji in the grid. A confirmation will show indicating it was copied to your clipboard.
